sections.title: 1. Saccades
sections.description: Saccade kinematics and internal signal cascade. Tests main sequence (amplitude–velocity), oblique trajectory linearity, double-step refractoriness, and the full Robinson cascade. The cascade figure also shows the cerebellar saccadic-suppression gates (pre- and post-delay), the pre- vs post-cascade slip + efference-copy comparison (cerebellum EC matched to the retinal cascade incl. the two-stage MN / MLF forward model), and the pursuit / VS / T-VOR drives during a saccade.

figures.title: Saccade Main Sequence
figures.description: Top: peak-velocity and duration main sequences (scatter + reference curves) vs amplitude. Bottom: eye position and velocity traces aligned to the target step, for amplitudes 0.5–20°.
figures.expected: Peak velocity within ±20% of 700(1−e^{−A/7}) (≈660 deg/s at 20°); duration grows roughly linearly with amplitude; velocity profiles are single-peaked and scale with amplitude.
figures.citation: Bahill et al. (1975) Science; Robinson (1975) J Neurophysiol

figures.title: Oblique Saccades (8-direction fan)
figures.description: 12° saccades from centre to 8 directions (45° apart), noiseless. Left: 2-D trajectories. Right: per-direction trajectory curvature.
figures.expected: Straight radial trajectories (curvature < ~5% of amplitude). Curvature is the readout of H/V component desynchrony, so it suffices on its own.
figures.citation: Smit et al. (1990) J Neurophysiol; van Gisbergen et al. (1985)

figures.title: Saccade Double-Step Refractoriness
figures.description: Double-step paradigm: target jumps 0→A/2 then A/2→A for A=10,20,30,40° after variable ISI (20–500 ms). Target shown dashed in same colour as eye trace. Short ISIs produce one amended saccade; longer ISIs produce two.
figures.expected: ISI < ~100 ms: 1 saccade (merged). ISI > ~150 ms: 2 separate saccades. Refractory period roughly constant across amplitudes (~150 ms).

figures.title: Saccade Signal Cascade
figures.description: Row-by-row signal flow for the 1°/5°/20°/40° saccades (noiseless) plus a 1° saccade WITH noise (last column, tinted): position, visual cascade + hold, accumulator/latch, residual error, burst, eye velocity, suppression gates, EC vs slip, and pursuit/VS drives.
figures.expected: e_held freezes at saccade onset; burst proportional to e_res; accumulator floor locks out the next saccade for ~270 ms; the noise column adds fixational drift + occasional microsaccades.
figures.citation: Robinson (1975) J Neurophysiol; Scudder et al. (2002); Kapoula, Robinson & Hain (1986)

sections.title: 2. VOR / OKR
sections.description: Vestibulo-ocular reflex and optokinetic response. Replicates Raphan et al. (1979) Fig.9: VOR in dark, OKN+OKAN, and VVOR. Slow-phase velocity shown as 0.5-s binned scatter (post-saccadic spikes averaged into the slow-phase trend). Tests post-rotatory and OKAN time constants, VVOR gain, and nystagmus waveform. Scene slip → VS goes through K_vor_direct·(saccadically-gated slip) + K_cereb_okr·(cerebellar EC correction); nodulus-uvula gravity dumping (K_cereb_nu) sets the velocity-storage TC.

figures.title: Raphan 1979 Fig. 9 Replication
figures.description: Panels A–F matching Raphan et al. (1979) Fig.9. Left col: SPV only. Right col: CUP (canal estimate), INT (velocity storage), SPV overlaid. A/B: VOR in dark. C/D: OKN+OKAN. E/F: VVOR (light→dark).
figures.expected: A: post-rot SPV TC 10–30 s. C: OKN gain~1, OKAN TC~20 s. E: VVOR gain>0.85 during rotation; post-rot TC similar to A. B/D/F: INT follows SPV; CUP decays at canal TC (~5 s).
figures.citation: Raphan, Matsuo & Cohen (1979) Exp Brain Res 35:229–248

figures.title: VOR / VVOR / OKR — Step Response
figures.description: Step / transient response — internal signals from the SAME simulations as the Raphan Fig.9 figure (no separate run); the Bode figures below cover the sinusoidal (frequency) response. Columns: VOR in dark, VVOR (rotation in a lit stationary scene), OKR (scene motion, head still). Rows: input → canal afferent / retinal slip → velocity storage → neural integrator → eye velocity, plus a fast-phase zoom (position + velocity). Vertical dashed line = stimulus off.
figures.expected: VOR: canal drives VS/NI, eye counter-rotates. VVOR: canal + OKR slip both feed VS, gaze stays stable. OKR: slip drives VS/NI, eye follows scene.

figures.title: VOR — Bode (body rotation)
figures.description: Sinusoidal head yaw sweep (≤30 deg/s, peak position capped at 20° so the target stays in range), NOISELESS. Eye velocity (SPV) ÷ head velocity in dark, light, light+target.
figures.expected: Light gain ≈ 1 across frequency (VVOR); dark gain rolls off (high-pass) below ~0.01 Hz — visible only with low_f=True.
figures.citation: Cohen, Matsuo & Raphan (1977); Raphan et al. (1979)

figures.title: OKR — Bode (full-field scene motion)
figures.description: Sinusoidal scene-velocity sweep (0.05–20 Hz, 20 deg/s), NOISELESS. Eye velocity (SPV) ÷ scene velocity for scene-only and scene+target. OKR is low-pass — gain rolls off above ~0.5 Hz; a stationary target suppresses it.
figures.expected: Gain ≈ 1 at low f, rolls off above ~0.5 Hz.

sections.title: 3. Gravity Estimator + T-VOR
sections.description: Canal-otolith interaction: OCR, OVAR, VOR tilt suppression, OCR vs tilt angle, somatogravic OCR frequency dependence, and translational VOR. These share a path — gravity/tilt and lateral translation both produce linear-acceleration components whose ocular effect is vergence/distance-dependent, so T-VOR lives here. Parameters: tau_grav=1.6666666666666667, K_gd=2.86, g_ocr=1.019 (Laurens & Angelaki 2011).

figures.title: Ocular Counterroll (OCR)
figures.description: Counter-roll traces for tilts [15.0, 30.0, 45.0, 90.0]° + SS torsion-vs-tilt scatter. g_ocr=1.02.
figures.expected: Torsion ≈ −G_OCR×G0×sin(θ). 30° → ≈−5.0°.

figures.title: OCR cascade (3 visual conditions)
figures.description: 30.0° roll tilt — OCR cascade across dark / scene-on / scene+target-on conditions, 15 s hold, noiseless.
figures.expected: Eye torsion stabilises at ≈-4.97° in all conditions. SPV row: -VS (dashed) and -canal FT (dotted) should bracket the measured SPV.

figures.title: OVAR — Off-Vertical Axis Rotation
figures.description: 60°/s rotation, tilt angles [10.0, 30.0, 60.0, 90.0]°. Replicates Laurens & Angelaki (2011) Fig 5.
figures.expected: SPV sinusoidally modulated at rotation period. Modulation amplitude ∝ sin(tilt). g_est oscillates ±G₀ sin(α). A small residual v_lin DC bias along the rotation axis ("screw direction") reflects the perceived sustained linear translation reported by subjects during prolonged OVAR (Denise et al. 1988; Wood 2002).
figures.citation: Laurens & Angelaki (2011) Exp Brain Res 210:407; Denise, Darlot, Droulez, Cohen, Berthoz (1988) Exp Brain Res 67:629 — perceived linear translation during OVAR; Wood (2002) J Vest Res 12:223 — tilt-translation discrimination

figures.title: VOR Tilt Suppression
figures.description: Upright 60°/s yaw; tilt [0.0, 30.0, 60.0, 90.0]° applied after stop. Replicates Laurens & Angelaki (2011) Fig 6.
figures.expected: Per-rotatory identical. TC decreases with post-stop tilt. 0°: TC ≈ τ_vs. 90°: TC ≈ τ_canal.

figures.title: Somatogravic OCR — Frequency Dependence
figures.description: Sinusoidal lateral translation at [0.03, 0.07, 0.15, 0.3, 0.7, 1.5] Hz, 2.0 m/s² peak. OCR via gravity estimator LP filter.
figures.expected: Corner frequency fc = K_grav/(2π) ≈ 0.040 Hz. OCR amplitude ∝ 1/√(1+(f/fc)²). Low f → peak ≈ 2.04°; high f → near 0°.

figures.title: T-VOR cascade — sway / heave / surge × LIT (scene only) / DARK
figures.description: Trapezoid head velocity (peak 20 cm/s, 200 ms ramps, 1.6 s plateau) along x (sway), y (heave), z (surge); target at 40 cm. Rows: stimulus, g_est, v_lin, eye position, eye SPV.
figures.expected: Sway → eye yaw counter-rotates (cross product); Heave → eye pitch counter-rotates; Surge → vergence rate (dot product · IPD/D²) drives vergence change. g_est should remain ≈ [0, 9.81, 0]. SPV in DARK measures pure vestibular T-VOR; LIT scene-only adds OKR / visual heading contribution.

figures.title: T-VOR — Lateral Bode (primary gain + torsion cross-effect)
figures.description: Sinusoidal interaural translation (20 cm/s, 0.1–4 Hz), NOISELESS, target at 40 cm. Top: horizontal eye-velocity gain vs the geometric ideal (V/d); middle: phase; bottom: cross-axis torsion (somatogravic / OCR coupling).
figures.expected: tVOR is high-pass — gain rises toward 1 at high frequency (otolith tilt/translation disambiguation). Cross torsion rolls off with frequency (somatogravic low-pass).
figures.citation: Paige & Tomko (1991); Angelaki & Hess (2001); Laurens & Angelaki (2011)

sections.title: 4. Smooth Pursuit
sections.description: Smooth pursuit and catch-up saccades. Tests velocity gain at multiple speeds, sinusoidal tracking, and the pursuit integrator + saccade interaction cascade. Pursuit input = K_pursuit_direct·(saccadically-gated retinal slip) + K_cereb_pu·(cerebellar EC correction); the closed-loop pursuit memory τ_eff ≈ 1.45 s (Smith predictor) despite the long pop-leak τ_pursuit = 40 s.

figures.title: Smooth Pursuit — Velocity Range
figures.description: Step-ramp target at 5, 10, 20, 40 deg/s. Blue: smooth pursuit enabled. Gray: saccades only (pursuit off). Rows: position, velocity, pursuit drive (total + integrator + phasic).
figures.expected: At 5–10 deg/s: pursuit tracks closely, steady-state gain > 0.8. At higher velocities: catch-up saccades + partial pursuit.

figures.title: Smooth Pursuit — Bode (frequency response)
figures.description: Sinusoidal target-velocity sweep (0.1–2 Hz, 10 deg/s peak), NOISELESS. Gain = eye-velocity (SPV) ÷ target-velocity; phase lag vs frequency.
figures.expected: Gain ≈ 1 at low f, −3 dB near ~1 Hz; phase lag grows with frequency.
figures.citation: Lisberger, Evinger, Johanson & Fuchs (1981) J Neurophysiol

figures.title: Smooth Pursuit Signal Cascade (Internal)
figures.description: Full signal chain for 20 deg/s pursuit: target ramps 0.2–2.0 s then holds. Pursuit integrator persists after target stop (τ ≈ 40 s). Rows: position, velocity, pursuit drive (total/integrator/phasic), visual error, saccade accumulator, burst, refractory state.
figures.expected: Phasic drive rises fast at onset, decays to 0 at steady state. After target stops at 2 s: phasic reverses (retinal slip now backward), integrator decays slowly — eye coasts then corrects with catch-up saccades. Integrator TC visible as slow drift in x_p post-stop.

sections.title: 5. Near response (vergence + accommodation)
sections.description: Merged near-response section. Basic midline vergence (noiseless) — reaches geometric target, version stays conjugate, latency/TC/overshoot. Vergence–saccade interaction (Zee SVBN facilitation). AC/A cross-link. Vergence and accommodation frequency responses (Bode). The vergence cascade is kept as an internal-signals debug figure (no metrics).

figures.title: Basic midline vergence (symmetric, noiseless)
figures.description: Symmetric convergence (3→0.3 m) and divergence (0.3→3 m) steps, NOISELESS. Left: vergence reaching geometric target. Right: version (conjugacy check).
figures.expected: Vergence reaches geometric target; version stays ≈0; rise TC ~0.2–1 s; small overshoot.
figures.citation: Mays (1984) J Neurophysiol; Rashbass & Westheimer (1961) J Physiol

figures.title: Accommodation step (monocular, noiseless)
figures.description: Far→near→far defocus step (0.17↔2.5 D), MONOCULAR (right eye occluded) so there is no disparity-driven vergence — accommodation isolated physiologically with the AC/A and CA/C cross-links left ON. Latency, time constant, SS gain (lag), peak velocity.
figures.expected: Latency ~0.3 s; rise TC ~0.2–0.5 s; SS gain ~0.85–0.95 (lag); exponential.
figures.citation: Del Águila-Carrasco et al. (2017) BioMed Res Int; Read et al. (2022) J Vision

figures.title: Vergence Main Sequence (peak velocity vs. amplitude)
figures.description: Peak vergence velocity for amplitudes 2–16°, both directions, with and without a concurrent version saccade. Left: symmetric (slow vergence only). Right: asymmetric (Zee SVBN burst active).
figures.expected: Symmetric: peak velocity grows roughly linearly with amplitude. Asymmetric: peak velocity 2–3× higher than symmetric (Zee facilitation). Convergence > divergence at every amplitude (asymmetric saturating gain).
figures.citation: Zee et al. (1992) J Neurophysiol; Rashbass & Westheimer (1961) J Physiol; Collewijn et al. (1988) J Physiol

figures.title: AC/A — lens-driven convergence
figures.description: +2 D binocular lens at 2 m, NOISELESS. With AC/A the extra accommodation drives convergence; with AC_A=0 vergence stays at geometric.
figures.expected: Accommodation rises ≥1 D; AC/A adds convergence above geometric; AC_A=0 control flat.

figures.title: Near-response short-term adaptation
figures.description: 32 s sustained near fixation (0.4 m) then open-loop (dark), control-subtracted against a far-throughout run with the identical dark transition. The gap between the two traces is the tonic adaptation induced by the near interval; it holds and decays slowly.
figures.expected: Adapted phoria + dark-focus shift toward the near demand (cond − control > 0); open-loop decay TC ~15–20 s (Hung 1992). The control subtraction removes the known dark over-convergence baseline.
figures.citation: Hung (1992) Ophthal Physiol Opt; Schor (1988) — imbalanced adaptation

figures.title: Vergence — Bode (frequency response)
figures.description: Sinusoidal depth/disparity demand (0.1–2 Hz, ±2° about 4°), NOISELESS. Gain = vergence ÷ demand; low-pass, BW ~1–2 Hz.
figures.expected: Gain ≈ 1 at low f, −3 dB near ~1 Hz; phase lag grows with frequency.

figures.title: Accommodation — Bode (frequency response)
figures.description: Sinusoidal accommodative demand (0.1–4 Hz, ±1 D about 2 D), NOISELESS. Gain = accommodation ÷ demand; low-pass, BW ~2 Hz.
figures.expected: Gain ≈ 1 at low f, −3 dB near ~2 Hz.

figures.title: Vergence Cascade (4 conditions)
figures.description: 8 rows × 4 cols. Cols: sym conv (3→0.3 m), sym div (0.3→3 m), asym R+conv (2→0.5 m, +10°), asym L+div (0.5→2 m, −10°). Rows: eye position, vergence, version, vergence velocity, OPN gate, integrators (x_verg + x_verg_tonic [H]), x_verg_copy, Zee SVBN burst.
figures.expected: Symmetric cols: version stays near 0; asymmetric: version follows target. Vergence settles at geometric target. SVBN fires only during saccades.
figures.citation: Zee et al. (1992) J Neurophysiol; Collewijn et al. (1988) J Physiol; Schor (1979) Vision Res

sections.title: 6. Fixation
sections.description: Fixational eye movements driven by different noise sources. Tests canal noise filtering, retinal position OU drift (microsaccades), and retinal velocity noise (pursuit-like drift).

figures.title: Fixational Eye Movements — Noise Source Comparison
figures.description: 5-column comparison: noiseless, canal noise only, retinal position OU drift, retinal velocity noise, and all three combined. Rows: eye position, velocity, saccade burst, noise signal.
figures.expected: Noiseless: eye stays at 0°. Canal noise: slow drift + rare microsaccades. Pos noise: OU drift → corrective microsaccades when error exceeds threshold. Vel noise: smooth pursuit-like drift.

sections.title: 7. Listing's Law
sections.description: Torsional constraint T = OCR − H·V·π/360: saccade plane scatter and OCR-driven correction saccade.

figures.title: Listing's Plane
figures.description: Saccades to a (H, V) grid; torsion measured at steady state. g_ocr=0 so OCR=0 and T_required = −H·V·π/360.
figures.expected: Scatter points lie on identity line; cross-sections linear with slope −V·π/360.
figures.citation: Tweed, Haslwanter & Fetter (1998) IOVS; van Rijn & van den Berg (1993) EBR

figures.title: OCR Torsional Correction Saccade
figures.description: Head tilt 30°; g_ocr=1.02. Torsional listing error drives a corrective saccade.
figures.expected: With saccades: torsion jumps to ≈-5.0° after accumulator delay (~80 ms × n_sacs). Without saccades: slow exponential rise with τ≈τ_i=25 s.

figures.title: Listing's Plane During Smooth Pursuit
figures.description: Sinusoidal H pursuit (15° at 0.5 Hz) at V=20°. vel_torsion demand added to NI keeps torsion on Listing's plane continuously.
figures.expected: Eye torsion tracks T_required = −H·20·π/360 in real time; Listing error RMS < 0.5°.
figures.citation: Tweed, Haslwanter & Fetter (1998) IOVS; van Rijn & van den Berg (1993) EBR

sections.title: 8. Final Common Pathway
sections.description: Motoneuron + cranial-nerve output stage. Traces the full command cascade — Robinson pulse/step decomposition of version and vergence drive through the 14 motoneurons (incl. the MLF internuclear AIN) to the 12 pull-only per-muscle nerves — over a 9-position gaze grid plus near/far vergence shifts. Exposes the [H,V,T]-command ↔ per-muscle recruitment mapping.

figures.title: Final Common Pathway — cascade over 9-position gaze + near/far vergence
figures.description: Stacked timecourse of the whole final common pathway for a center-out-center 9-position gaze sequence (±30° at 1 m) followed by near (0.25 m) and far (4 m) depth shifts. Version drive is decomposed Robinson pulse/step (NI bilateral pops + τp·burst feed-through); vergence drive is decomposed step (verg_fast+tonic), pulse (direct path) and AC/A cross-link, all in H/V/T. The command then flows through the 14 signed motoneurons (incl. CN4 and the MLF internuclear AIN) to the 12 pull-only per-muscle nerve drives, split by eye.
figures.expected: Eye reaches each gaze target; ocular vergence rides ~3.7° at 1 m, ~14.6° at near and ~0.9° at far (V/T vergence ≈ 0 in this symmetric paradigm). Each saccade is an NI step + burst pulse; the conjugate [H,V,T] command spreads across the recti/obliques per eye. Nerve drive stays pull-only (≥0) via reciprocal co-contraction.
